content.text: How the Audit Effect Works

The Audit effect evaluates resources against the policy condition and records non-compliant resources in the Azure Policy compliance report — but it does not block the operation. The resource is created or updated successfully regardless of whether it matches the condition. The effect writes a PolicyStateChanged event to the activity log and updates the compliance dashboard to show the resource as Non-Compliant. Audit is the correct effect when you want visibility into a configuration state before deciding whether to enforce a deny, or when blocking operations would be too disruptive and remediation is expected to happen through a separate process.

attrs.alt: Audit effect non-blocking evaluation flow diagram

content.text: Audit vs Deny: The Critical Difference

Audit and Deny use the same condition syntax in policyRule.if — the only difference is the policyRule.then.effect value. With deny, matching resources are blocked at request time and never created. With audit, matching resources are allowed through and marked as non-compliant in reporting. Existing resources become non-compliant the next time they are evaluated (evaluation runs on creation, update, and on a periodic scan every 24 hours). There is no 403 error, no deployment failure, and no developer-visible signal at creation time — the non-compliance shows up only in the compliance dashboard.
Dimension
Audit
Deny
Request outcome
Succeeds — resource created/updated
Fails — 403 RequestDisallowedByPolicy
Developer impact
None at deployment time
Deployment blocked until configuration is fixed
Compliance state
NonCompliant (visible in dashboard)
Prevented from existing — no compliance state needed
Existing resources
Marked NonCompliant on next evaluation
Not affected — already exist; future updates blocked
Use case
Visibility, gradual rollout, reporting
Hard enforcement, security requirements
Reverting
No remediation needed for existing resources
Existing non-compliant resources persist; new ones blocked
Audit Policy Definition

An audit policy definition is structurally identical to a deny definition with one word changed. The best practice is to use an effect parameter so the same definition can be assigned with audit, deny, or disabled without creating a new definition.
What Audit Reports On

An audit policy generates compliance data for every resource that matches the condition at the assignment scope. The compliance report shows: the total number of resources evaluated, how many are compliant, how many are non-compliant, and how many are exempt. This data is available in the Azure portal, via az policy state list, and via Resource Graph queries — and it powers the Microsoft Defender for Cloud regulatory compliance view.

# List all non-compliant resources for an audit assignment
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'audit-https-storage' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Type:resourceType,State:complianceState}' \
  -o table

# Get compliance summary (counts by state)
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'audit-https-storage'" \
  --query 'value[0].results.{Compliant:compliantResources,NonCompliant:nonCompliantResources,Exempt:exemptResources}'

# Get compliance percentage
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'audit-https-storage'" \
  --query 'value[0].results.resourceDetails[?complianceState==`Compliant`].count | [0]'

# Trigger a compliance scan (refresh state without waiting 24h)
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID

# List non-compliant resources across ALL audit assignments in a subscription
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Assignment:policyAssignmentName,Action:policyDefinitionAction}' \
  -o table
Audit as a Pre-Enforcement Gate

The canonical use of audit is as the first phase of a two-phase rollout: deploy with audit, measure the blast radius (how many resources would be blocked), remediate the existing non-compliant resources, then switch the effect to deny. This pattern ensures that switching to deny does not immediately break workloads that were deployed before the policy existed.

# Phase 1: Deploy with audit effect
az policy assignment create \
  --name require-https-storage \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --policy $DEF_ID \
  --params '{"effect":{"value":"audit"}}'

# Wait for evaluation cycle or trigger manually
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID

# Measure blast radius: how many resources would be blocked if we switched to deny?
NON_COMPLIANT=$(az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'require-https-storage' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query 'length(@)' -o tsv)
echo "Would block $NON_COMPLIANT existing resources if switched to deny now"

# Get list of non-compliant resources for remediation
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'require-https-storage' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].resourceId' -o tsv > non-compliant-storage.txt

# Remediate each resource (enable HTTPS-only on storage accounts)
while IFS= read -r RES_ID; do
  SA_NAME=$(az resource show --ids "$RES_ID" --query 'name' -o tsv)
  RG=$(az resource show --ids "$RES_ID" --query 'resourceGroup' -o tsv)
  az storage account update --name $SA_NAME --resource-group $RG --https-only true
done < non-compliant-storage.txt

# Phase 2: Trigger rescan and verify all compliant
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID

# Phase 3: Switch to deny after all resources are compliant
az policy assignment update \
  --name require-https-storage \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --params '{"effect":{"value":"deny"}}'

Never switch a policy from audit to deny without checking the non-compliant count first. A large count means existing workloads depend on the non-compliant configuration — switching blindly to deny will block updates to those resources and break their CI/CD pipelines on the next deployment.
AuditIfNotExists vs Audit

The Audit effect evaluates the resource itself — checking its own properties. AuditIfNotExists (AINE) evaluates whether a companion resource exists alongside the target resource. Use Audit when the compliance condition is entirely within the resource's own properties. Use AuditIfNotExists when compliance depends on the presence of a related resource (a diagnostic setting, a VM extension, a private endpoint). For example: auditing whether a storage account has HTTPS enabled is Audit; auditing whether a storage account has a diagnostic setting pointing to a Log Analytics workspace is AuditIfNotExists.
Scenario
Use
Reason
Storage account must have HTTPS-only = true
Audit
Property is on the resource itself
VM must have Log Analytics agent extension
AuditIfNotExists
Extension is a child resource, not a property of the VM
Key Vault must have soft delete enabled
Audit
softDeleteRetentionInDays is a Key Vault property
Key Vault must have a diagnostic setting
AuditIfNotExists
Diagnostic settings are a separate child resource
NSG must not allow inbound port 22
Audit
Security rules are array properties of the NSG
Subscription must have a Security Center contact
AuditIfNotExists
Contact is a separate resource under the subscription
Terraform: Audit Policy Assignment
Resource Graph: Querying Audit Compliance Data

// All non-compliant resources for audit policies (not deny — those never exist)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where properties.policyDefinitionAction == 'audit'
| project
    resourceId = tostring(properties.resourceId),
    resourceType = tostring(properties.resourceType),
    policyAssignment = tostring(properties.policyAssignmentName),
    subscriptionId
| order by policyAssignment asc, resourceType asc

// Compliance trend: count by state and day (last 7 days)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.policyAssignmentName == 'audit-https-storage'
| summarize
    NonCompliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'),
    Compliant = countif(properties.complianceState == 'Compliant')
| project NonCompliant, Compliant,
    CompliancePercent = round(100.0 * Compliant / (Compliant + NonCompliant), 1)

// Top 10 resource types with most non-compliant resources across all audit policies
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'Microsoft.PolicyInsights/PolicyStates'
| where properties.complianceState == 'NonCompliant'
| where properties.policyDefinitionAction == 'audit'
| summarize Count = count() by resourceType = tostring(properties.resourceType)
| top 10 by Count desc
AuditIfNotExists: Checking for Companion Resources

AuditIfNotExists (AINE) is a variant of Audit that checks whether a companion resource exists alongside the target resource. The policy evaluates a primary resource (e.g. a VM), then looks for a child or related resource of a specified type (e.g. a VM extension). If the companion is absent or does not match the existence condition, the primary resource is marked non-compliant. Like Audit, AINE never blocks the operation — it only records compliance state.

The details.type field specifies what companion resource to look for. The details.existenceCondition specifies the property values that must be present on the companion. If no companion of that type exists, or if one exists but fails the existenceCondition, the primary resource is non-compliant. The companion resource search uses the primary resource's scope — for a VM extension, it searches child extensions of that VM.

# Common AuditIfNotExists patterns:

# 1. Audit VMs without a specific extension
#    details.type: Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions
#    existenceCondition: extension.publisher == expected value

# 2. Audit storage accounts without a diagnostic setting
#    details.type: microsoft.insights/diagnosticSettings
#    existenceCondition: logs contain the required categories

# 3. Audit Key Vaults without a private endpoint connection
#    details.type: Microsoft.Network/privateEndpointConnections
#    existenceCondition: privateLinkServiceConnectionState.status == Approved

# 4. Audit subscriptions without a Security Center contact
#    details.type: Microsoft.Security/securityContacts
#    existenceCondition: properties.email is not empty

# Check AINE compliance — non-compliant means companion is missing
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'audit-vm-law-agent' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query '[].{VM:resourceId,State:complianceState}' \
  -o table

# The non-compliant resource is the VM (primary), not the missing extension (companion)
# To see which extension is missing, check the VM's extensions directly:
az vm extension list \
  --vm-name $VM_NAME \
  --resource-group $RG \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Publisher:publisher,State:provisioningState}' \
  -o table
Audit Compliance in Defender for Cloud

Audit policy assignments feed directly into Microsoft Defender for Cloud's Regulatory Compliance dashboard and Secure Score. Built-in initiatives like Azure Security Benchmark and CIS controls use a mix of Audit and AuditIfNotExists policies to populate the regulatory compliance report. Non-compliant resources from audit policies appear as recommendations in Defender for Cloud, with suggested remediation steps.

# View compliance against Azure Security Benchmark initiative
az policy state summarize \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policySetDefinitionId eq '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8'" \
  --query 'value[].{Control:policyDefinition.displayName,NonCompliant:results.nonCompliantResources}' \
  -o table

# List all audit policies currently assigned to a subscription
az policy assignment list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --include-inherited \
  --query "[?policyDefinitionId != null]" -o json | \
  jq -r '.[] | select(.enforcementMode == "Default") | .displayName' | sort

# Export non-compliant resources to CSV for remediation tracking
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --select 'resourceId,resourceType,policyAssignmentName,policyDefinitionAction,timestamp' \
  --query '[].{Resource:resourceId,Type:resourceType,Policy:policyAssignmentName,Effect:policyDefinitionAction,Timestamp:timestamp}' \
  -o table > non-compliant-$(date +%Y%m%d).txt

# Count by policy definition action (audit vs auditIfNotExists)
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query 'length(@)'
Operational Patterns: Using Audit for Governance Visibility

Beyond pre-enforcement testing, Audit policies provide ongoing visibility into configuration drift in long-lived environments. Resources that were compliant when deployed can drift out of compliance as teams make ad-hoc changes. A suite of audit policies provides a continuous compliance signal that can trigger alerts when the non-compliant count increases above a threshold.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# compliance-alert.sh — alert when non-compliant count exceeds threshold
SUB='11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111'
POLICY_ASSIGNMENT='audit-https-storage'
THRESHOLD=10

# Trigger a fresh scan
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB --no-wait
sleep 60  # Allow scan to partially complete

NON_COMPLIANT=$(az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq '$POLICY_ASSIGNMENT' and complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query 'length(@)' -o tsv)

echo "Non-compliant count: $NON_COMPLIANT (threshold: $THRESHOLD)"

if [ "$NON_COMPLIANT" -gt "$THRESHOLD" ]; then
  echo "ALERT: Non-compliant count $NON_COMPLIANT exceeds threshold $THRESHOLD"
  # Send to monitoring system, Teams webhook, PagerDuty, etc.
  # Example: post to Teams webhook
  curl -X POST "$TEAMS_WEBHOOK_URL" \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -d "{\"text\": \"Azure Policy Alert: $NON_COMPLIANT storage accounts are not HTTPS-only (threshold: $THRESHOLD). Subscription: $SUB\"}"
fi

# This script can be run as an Azure Automation runbook on a schedule,
# or as a Logic App triggered by an Event Grid policy state change event.

Azure Policy emits PolicyStateChanged events to Event Grid when a resource's compliance state changes. Subscribing to these events with a Logic App or Azure Function allows real-time alerting without polling. The event payload includes the resource ID, the policy assignment, and the new compliance state.
Summary

The Audit effect evaluates resources against the policy condition and records non-compliant resources in the compliance dashboard without blocking the operation. Resources are created and updated normally — there is no 403 error, no deployment failure, and no developer-visible impact at creation time. Compliance state is updated synchronously on create/update and asynchronously via the 24-hour evaluation cycle. Audit is the first phase of a safe enforcement rollout: deploy with audit, measure the blast radius (how many existing resources are non-compliant), remediate them, then switch to deny. AuditIfNotExists covers the different scenario of checking for companion resource existence. Use Resource Graph kusto queries or az policy state list to extract and analyze compliance data from audit assignments.
